macro_segments.title: Introduction to Ketubot obligations: The transition from father's to husband's house and the framework of marital rights and responsibilities

micro_segments.title: The masechet's focus on women's dependency and the progression from father's house to husband's house
The distinction between erusin and nissuin and when Ketubot obligations take effect
Overview of the three primary reciprocal obligations: feeding, redeeming from captivity, and burial

macro_segments.title: The reciprocal obligations of the Ketubah: which party is entitled and the logic of exchange

micro_segments.title: Teaching of the Rabbis establishing obligations in exchange for rights: food for earnings, redeeming for fruit, burial for dowry
Gemara's question about which party is protected: the importance of framing obligations as rights or responsibilities
The logic of allowing the husband to benefit from fruit: ensuring willingness to redeem from captivity
Modern application: the Kolech organization's approach to reframing the Ketubah as a woman's choice

macro_segments.title: Matching obligations to rights: the principle of common for common and uncommon for uncommon

micro_segments.title: Abaye's principle that common things are matched with common things and uncommon with uncommon
Food and earnings as everyday obligations, burial and dowry as one-time events, redemption and fruit earnings as variable amounts
Discussion of historical context: dowry as common, fruit-bearing property as less common

macro_segments.title: The Ketubah text and document: standard formulas, obligations mentioned, and historical changes in protection

micro_segments.title: Reading the Ketubah text: formula of marriage, husband's commitments including honor, work, and sustenance
Obligations listed: sustenance, clothing, and sexual relations; those not explicitly mentioned such as healing and burial
The fixed dowry amount and tosefes: how the amount was standardized to protect the wife's interests
Historical development: replacement of 200 zuz with 100 kesef tzaruf by the Rishonim for better protection

macro_segments.title: Biblical versus Rabbinic obligations: which marital duties are from the Torah and which are Rabbinic enactments

micro_segments.title: Rava's question: whether sustenance is a biblical obligation or rabbinic
Braita from Exodus: three obligations derived from Scripture—sustenance, clothing, and conjugal relations
Rabbi Eliezer's interpretation: only clothing is biblical; food and sex are understood differently
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov's approach: parsing the three words to derive appropriate clothing obligations
Alternative views on She'erah: sex as flesh-related obligation and the principle of kiruv basar (coming together of bodies)
Consensus position: only conjugal relations are biblical; food and clothing are rabbinic obligations
Ramban's interpretation: three dimensions of marital sexuality—union, appropriate environment, and regularity

macro_segments.title: Burial obligations and standards: whose status determines the funeral expenses and the principle of rising but not falling

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's minimum requirement: all deceased women receive proper burial with musicians and a wailer
Gemara's distinction: Tanna Kamma allows lower standard based on family practice; Rabbi Yehuda requires higher standard
Principle of olah immo ve-einah yoredes immo: she ascends with his status but does not descend with his poverty
Application during marriage versus after death: whether higher standard applies after she predeceases him

macro_segments.title: Support obligations when the husband is absent or incapacitated: Beit Din's intervention and limits of obligation

micro_segments.title: Case of deranged husband: Beit Din sells property to support wife and children from his estate
Case of conscious abandonment: Beit Din supports wife (legal obligation) but not children (no legal obligation)
Distinction between legal obligation and presumed intent: why we assume he wants to care for wife but not children when absent
Additional matters (davar acher): jewelry and charity in cases of incapacity versus conscious abandonment

macro_segments.title: Burial when the husband predeceases or is abroad: estate obligations and refusal of burial

micro_segments.title: Husband abroad when wife dies: Beit Din uses estate property to bury her according to his status
Status applied: use the higher standard between husband's and wife's family status
Deathbed refusal to be buried from estate: cannot refuse to have heirs bear funeral costs; community obligation upheld
Principle: cannot enrich heirs by refusing burial at estate expense; rightfully shifts burden to community

topical_tags.term: Nichsei Milug (property where wife retains title)
T'nai Ketubah (stipulations of ongoing marital obligations)
She'er (flesh) - interpreted as sustenance or sexual relations
Kiruv Basar (coming together of flesh/marital intimacy)
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov's clothing-focused approach
Ramban's three dimensions of marital sexuality
Olah immo ve-einah yoredes immo (ascends with his status, does not descend with poverty)
Abaye's principle: matzui l'matzui v'she'eino matzui l'she'eino matzui (common for common, uncommon for uncommon)
Kesef Tzaruf (refined silver - substitute for zuz)
Beit Din intervention in property and support
Rashi's interpretation of She'er Kesut ve-Onah
Ramban's interpretation in commentary to Torah
